IoT in bridge and highway structural health monitoring
======================================================

Abstract
--------

The integration of the Internet of Things (IoT) into bridge and highway structural health monitoring (SHM) systems represents a transformative advancement in infrastructure management and public safety. IoT-enabled SHM leverages interconnected sensors, wireless communication, and real-time data analytics to continuously monitor critical parameters such as strain, vibration, tilt, temperature, and displacement in bridges and highways. These intelligent systems enable early detection of structural anomalies, deterioration, and potential failures, thereby facilitating proactive maintenance and extending the lifespan of infrastructure assets. By providing real-time insights and alerts, IoT-based SHM enhances decision-making for engineers and transportation authorities, reduces maintenance costs, and minimizes the risk of catastrophic failures. Moreover, the integration with cloud platforms and AI algorithms supports predictive analytics, enabling a shift from reactive to condition-based maintenance models. This paper explores the architecture, benefits, challenges, and future prospects of IoT in structural health monitoring, emphasizing its role in building resilient and smart transportation infrastructure.

IoT in bridge and highway structural health monitoring 

Author: Olatunji Isreal 

Da te: 27 th February 2022 

Abstract:

The integration of the Internet of Things (IoT) into bridge and highway structural health 

monitoring (SHM) systems represents a transformative advancement in infrastructure 

management and public safety. IoT-enabled SHM leverages interconnected sensors, wireless 

communication, and real-time data analytics to continuously monitor critical parameters such as 

strain, vibration, tilt, temperature, and displacement in bridges and highways. These intelligent 

systems enable early detection of structural anomalies, deterioration, and potential failures, 

thereby facilitating proactive maintenance and extending the lifespan of infrastructure assets. By 

providing real-time insights and alerts, IoT-based SHM enhances decision-making for engineers 

and transportation authorities, reduces maintenance costs, and minimizes the risk of catastrophic 

failures. Moreover, the integration with cloud platforms and AI algorithms supports predictive 

analytics, enabling a shift from reactive to condition-based maintenance models. This paper 

explores the architecture, benefits, challenges, and future prospects of IoT in structural health 

monitoring, emphasizing its role in building resilient and smart transportation infrastructure. 

Introduction

A. Importance of Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) for Transportation Safety and 

Longevity

Bridges and highways are vital components of modern transportation networks, enabling 

mobility, economic activity, and social connectivity. Ensuring their structural integrity is critical 

to public safety, infrastructure resilience, and economic sustainability. Structural Health 

Monitoring (SHM) serves as a proactive approach to assess the condition of infrastructure, detect 

early signs of deterioration, and support timely maintenance interventions. Effective SHM not 

only prevents catastrophic failures and loss of life but also extends the service life of assets and 

optimizes lifecycle costs. 

B. Challenges in Traditional SHM Methods

Conventional SHM techniques often rely on manual inspections and periodic assessments, which 

are labor-intensive, time-consuming, and limited in frequency. These methods may miss 

emerging structural issues between inspections, particularly in hard-to-access or remote 

locations. Additionally, subjective assessments and inconsistent reporting can reduce the 

reliability of condition evaluations, leading to delayed repairs or over-conservative maintenance 

strategies. The limitations of traditional SHM pose a significant risk to infrastructure 

performance and long-term safety. 

C. Role of IoT in Transforming Infrastructure Maintenance and Safety

The advent of the Internet of Things (IoT) is revolutionizing SHM by enabling continuous, real-

time monitoring of structural conditions through a network of smart sensors and communication 

technologies. IoT-based SHM systems collect data on stress, strain, vibration, displacement, and 

environmental factors, providing high-resolution insights into infrastructure performance. These 

systems enhance the accuracy and timeliness of diagnostics, support predictive maintenance 

through data analytics and machine learning, and reduce reliance on manual inspections. By 

integrating with cloud platforms and digital twins, IoT is driving a shift toward intelligent, data-

driven infrastructure management that prioritizes safety, efficiency, and sustainability. 

Overview of IoT in Structural Health Monitor ing

A. Definition and Scope of IoT in SHM

The Internet of Things (IoT) in Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) refers to the deployment of 

interconnected sensing and communication technologies to monitor the physical condition and 

performance of infrastructure assets such as bridges and highways. IoT-enabled SHM systems 

collect, transmit, and analyze structural data in real time, enabling continuous assessment and 

early detection of faults or degradation. The scope of IoT in SHM encompasses not only real-

time monitoring but also predictive maintenance, automated alerts, and integration with broader 

smart city and intelligent transportation systems. 

B. Components of an IoT-Based SHM System

1.Smart Sensors

A variety of sensors are used to capture structural and environmental data, including: 

o Strain gauges– for detecting deformations and load responses. 

o Vibration sensors– to identify changes in dynamic behavior, which may indicate 

damage or instability. 

o Tilt sensors– to measure angular displacement or shifting. 

o Corrosion sensors– for monitoring material degradation in metal components. 

o Temperature and humidity sensors– to track environmental influences on 

structural integrity. 

2.Data Acquisition Units (DAUs)

These units collect raw data from sensors and convert it into digital signals for 

processing. They often include signal conditioning circuits, analog-to-digital converters, 

and basic data filtering mechanisms. 

3.Communication Networks

Reliable data transmission is critical in IoT-based SHM. Common technologies include: 

o 5G– for high-speed, low-latency communication. 

o Low-Power Wide-Area Networks (LPWAN)– for long-range, energy-efficient 

transmission in remote or rural areas. 

o Narrowband IoT (NB-IoT)– optimized for low-bandwidth, low-power 

applications with deep indoor penetration. 

4.Cloud and Edge Computing Platforms

Cloud computing enables centralized storage, processing, and analysis of large-scale 

SHM data. Edge computing complements this by processing data closer to the source, 

reducing latency and bandwidth usage while enabling real-time decision-making at the 

local level. 

5.Data Analytics and AI Algorithms

Advanced analytics, including machine learning and pattern recognition, are applied to 

detect anomalies, predict failures, and optimize maintenance schedules. AI models 

improve over time by learning from historical and real-time data, enhancing their 

predictive accuracy. 

C. Benefits of Real-Time, Continuous Monitoring

IoT-based SHM offers substantial advantages over traditional inspection methods, including: 

•Early Fault Detection: Continuous monitoring ensures that even minor structural 

changes are detected before they evolve into serious issues. 

•Enhanced Safety: Real-time alerts enable timely interventions, preventing accidents and 

minimizing risks to human life. 

•Cost-Effective Maintenance: Predictive insights help allocate maintenance resources 

efficiently, reducing unnecessary repairs and extending asset lifespans. 

•Reduced Downtime: Infrastructure remains operational longer with fewer unscheduled 

closures for inspection or emergency repairs. 

•Data-Driven Decision Making: Engineers and policymakers gain access to 

comprehensive, high-resolution data to support evidence-based infrastructure planning 

and investment. 

Key Applications in Bridges and High ways

The deployment of IoT in Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) has enabled a broad range of 

applications critical to ensuring the integrity, safety, and performance of bridges and highways. 

These applications rely on precise, continuous data collection and analysis to detect issues early 

and inform timely interventions. 

A. Load and Stress Monitoring

IoT-based sensors such as strain gauges and load cells measure the stress and strain experienced 

by structural components under traffic loads, environmental forces, and material aging. This data 

helps engineers assess whether structures are operating within safe limits and evaluate the long-

term effects of dynamic and static loads. 

B. Crack Detection and Growth Tracking

High-resolution sensors and acoustic emission monitoring systems detect the initiation and 

propagation of cracks in concrete, steel, and composite materials. By continuously tracking crack 

width and depth over time, maintenance teams can prioritize repairs before structural integrity is 

compromised. 

C. Corrosion and Material Degradation Monitoring

Corrosion sensors embedded in steel reinforcements or surface-mounted on metallic elements 

track chemical and moisture conditions that lead to corrosion. Monitoring chloride ion 

concentration, pH levels, and electrochemical potential enables early identification of corrosive 

environments and degradation trends, particularly in coastal or industrial regions. 

D. Vibration and Displacement Analysis

Accelerometers and tilt sensors are used to capture vibration signatures and structural movement. 

Changes in modal frequencies or amplitude can indicate stiffness loss, damage progression, or 

foundation shifts. Displacement sensors also track bending, tilting, or sagging in bridge decks 

and highway pavements under various conditions. 

E. Impact and Fatigue Damage Analysis

IoT systems equipped with impact sensors and fatigue monitoring algorithms assess the effects 

of repetitive loading, vehicle collisions, or overloading events. Data gathered helps identify high-

stress zones susceptible to fatigue cracks and informs the scheduling of inspections or 

reinforcements in those areas. 

F. Seismic and Extreme Weather Event Monitoring

In earthquake-prone or climate-vulnerable regions, IoT SHM systems provide real-time data 

during seismic or extreme weather events such as floods, storms, or heatwaves. Seismometers 

and weather sensors monitor structural response to such events, allowing for immediate safety 

assessments, evacuation decisions, and post-event damage analysis. 

Together, these applications illustrate the powerful capabilities of IoT-enabled SHM in 

transforming how bridges and highways are maintained—shifting from reactive maintenance to 

proactive, data-informed infrastructure management. 

IoT Technologies and Tools in Use

The effectiveness of IoT-based Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) in bridges and highways is 

largely driven by advanced technologies and tools that enable accurate, scalable, and intelligent 

monitoring. These innovations are essential for capturing diverse types of structural data, 

ensuring efficient data transmission, and enabling real-time analysis and decision-making. 

A. Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs)

Wireless Sensor Networks consist of spatially distributed smart sensors that communicate 

wirelessly to monitor structural parameters such as strain, tilt, vibration, and temperature. WSNs 

eliminate the need for extensive wiring, making them cost-effective and easier to deploy across 

large or remote infrastructure. These networks can be self-organizing and support real-time data 

collection, aggregation, and transmission to central servers or cloud platforms. 

B. MEMS-Based Sensors (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems)

MEMS technology enables the development of compact, low-power sensors capable of detecting 

mechanical and physical changes with high precision. Common MEMS sensors in SHM include 

accelerometers, gyroscopes, and pressure sensors. Their small size and low energy consumption 

make them ideal for long-term deployment in harsh environments, including embedded systems 

within bridge decks and road surfaces. 

C. Edge AI for Real-Time Anomaly Detection

Edge computing combined with artificial intelligence (Edge AI) brings computation closer to the 

data source, enabling real-time processing of sensor data without relying heavily on cloud 

infrastructure. AI models deployed on edge devices can detect structural anomalies—such as 

unusual vibration patterns or rapid stress increases—instantaneously and trigger alerts or 

automated safety responses. This significantly reduces latency and improves system 

responsiveness, especially in critical scenarios. 

D. Digital Twins for Predictive Maintenance

A digital twin is a virtual model of a physical infrastructure asset that mirrors its real-time 

condition using live data from IoT sensors. Digital twins allow engineers to simulate structural 

behavior, predict potential failures, and test maintenance strategies before implementing them in 

the real world. This predictive capability supports proactive maintenance, reduces downtime, and 

extends the operational life of bridges and highways. 

E. Drone-Integrated IoT for Visual and Sensor Data Fusion

Drones equipped with IoT sensors and high-resolution cameras enhance SHM by providing 

aerial inspections of hard-to-reach areas such as bridge undersides, tall piers, and expansive 

highway segments. These drones collect both visual imagery and sensor data (e.g., thermal, 

LiDAR, ultrasonic), which can be fused with ground-based sensor inputs to generate 

comprehensive structural assessments. Drone-based SHM is especially valuable for rapid post-

disaster assessments and routine inspections in inaccessible or hazardous locations. 

Data Analytics and Predictive Mainten ance

IoT-driven Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) generates large volumes of real-time data, 

which must be processed, analyzed, and interpreted effectively to deliver actionable insights. 

Advanced data analytics and predictive maintenance technologies transform raw sensor data into 

valuable intelligence that supports timely interventions and informed decision-making. 

A. Real-Time Data Visualization Dashboards

Interactive dashboards are essential tools for monitoring the health of infrastructure assets in real 

time. These platforms aggregate data from various IoT sensors and display it through intuitive 

visual interfaces, including graphs, heat maps, and status indicators. Engineers and asset 

managers can quickly assess structural conditions, monitor performance trends, and identify 

critical issues at a glance. Dashboards also support custom thresholds and filters to focus on 

specific parameters such as stress, vibration, or temperature changes. 

B. Machine Learning for Pattern Recognition and Anomaly Detection

Machine learning (ML) algorithms are employed to analyze large, complex datasets and identify 

patterns that may indicate the early stages of structural damage. Supervised and unsupervised 

learning models can detect deviations from normal structural behavior, such as abnormal strain 

levels or unusual vibration frequencies. These models continuously learn from new data, 

improving their accuracy over time and reducing false positives, which enhances trust in 

automated monitoring systems. 

C. Predictive Models for Failure Forecasting

Predictive analytics uses historical and real-time sensor data to estimate the remaining useful life 

(RUL) of structural components and forecast potential failure points. Techniques such as 

regression analysis, time series forecasting, and deep learning are applied to anticipate 

deterioration trends based on load cycles, environmental conditions, and material fatigue. These 

forecasts enable proactive maintenance planning and budget allocation, reducing the risk of 

unexpected failures and extending infrastructure lifespan. 

D. Automated Alerts and Maintenance Scheduling

IoT systems can be programmed to issue automated alerts when sensor readings exceed 

predefined safety thresholds or when anomaly detection algorithms flag critical issues. These 

alerts are sent to maintenance teams via SMS, email, or platform notifications, ensuring rapid 

response to potential threats. In more advanced systems, alert-triggered workflows can initiate 

automated maintenance scheduling, equipment dispatch, or inspection task generation within 

asset management systems, streamlining operations and minimizing downtime. 

Case Studies and Implementations

Th e real-world application of IoT in Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) demonstrates its 

effectiveness in enhancing the safety, performance, and management of critical infrastructure. 

The following case studies highlight successful implementations and potential opportunities for 

IoT-based SHM in bridges and highways globally. 

A. Golden Gate Bridge (USA): Sensor-Based Fatigue Monitoring

The Golden Gate Bridge in San Francisco has implemented an advanced SHM system with over 

300 sensors deployed to monitor structural behavior, particularly focusing on fatigue and stress 

induced by traffic loads and environmental conditions. These sensors measure strain, vibration, 

and displacement, helping engineers assess the long-term impacts of dynamic loading on the 

bridge’s steel components. Real-time data collection and analysis allow for early detection of 

fatigue-related risks, enabling timely maintenance and extending the bridge’s operational life.

B. Millau Viaduct (France): Structural Load and Wind Monitoring

The Millau Viaduct, one of the tallest bridges in the world, incorporates an IoT-enabled SHM 

system to monitor wind speeds, thermal expansion, and structural loading. Equipped with an 

array of anemometers, strain gauges, and temperature sensors, the system ensures the bridge's 

stability under varying weather and traffic conditions. Data from these sensors is transmitted to a 

central control center, where engineers use predictive analytics to manage risks and optimize 

maintenance schedules in one of the most complex structural environments in Europe. 

C. Lagos Third Mainland Bridge (Nigeria): Potential for IoT Retrofit

The Third Mainland Bridge, the longest in Nigeria, is a vital transportation link in Lagos but has 

experienced structural deterioration due to aging, traffic stress, and environmental factors. While 

traditional inspections and periodic maintenance have been applied, the bridge presents a 

significant opportunity for IoT-based SHM retrofit. Implementing a sensor network to monitor 

crack propagation, vibration, corrosion, and displacement could enable proactive maintenance, 

reduce the risk of sudden closures, and improve traffic management. Retrofitting this bridge with 

IoT technology would serve as a model for smart infrastructure transformation in sub-Saharan 

Africa. 

D. China’s Smart Highway Infrastructure Pilots

China has launched several smart highway initiatives that integrate IoT, 5G, and AI technologies 

for SHM and traffic management. In provinces such as Zhejiang and Hebei, highways are 

embedded with fiber-optic sensors, RFID systems, and edge computing units to monitor stress, 

pavement condition, vehicle flow, and environmental factors. These pilot projects aim to support 

autonomous driving, real-time maintenance alerts, and enhanced roadway resilience. The 

successful implementation of these systems demonstrates the scalability of IoT SHM in national 

infrastructure networks and provides valuable insights for global smart transportation strategies. 

Challenges and Limitations

While IoT-based Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) offers transformative benefits for bridges 

and highways, its implementation is not without challenges. Technical, economic, and 

operational barriers must be addressed to ensure successful deployment, scalability, and 

sustainability of these systems. 

A. Power Supply and Energy Harvesting for Remote Sensors

One of the key challenges in IoT SHM is ensuring continuous power supply to sensors, 

especially in remote or hard-to-reach locations where frequent battery replacement is impractical. 

Although energy harvesting technologies—such as solar, vibration, or thermal energy—are being 

explored, they are often limited by environmental conditions and efficiency constraints. 

Developing low-power sensors and reliable energy harvesting methods is essential for long-term, 

maintenance-free operation. 

B. Sensor Calibration and Lifespan

Maintaining the accuracy and reliability of sensor data over time requires periodic calibration, 

which can be logistically difficult and costly. Sensor drift, exposure to harsh environmental 

conditions, and material degradation can affect performance and lifespan. Without regular 

calibration and health checks, the integrity of the monitoring system may be compromised, 

leading to false readings or missed anomalies. 

C. Data Security and Integrity

The transmission and storage of critical infrastructure data pose cybersecurity risks. 

Unauthorized access, data tampering, or system hacking can lead to misinformation or system 

malfunction, with potentially severe safety implications. Ensuring robust encryption, 

authentication protocols, and secure cloud infrastructure is vital to protect sensitive SHM data 

and maintain trust in automated decision-making systems. 

D. Integration with Legacy Infrastructure

Many existing bridges and highways were not designed with digital integration in mind, making 

retrofitting with IoT components technically complex. Structural modifications, compatibility 

with modern sensors, and physical constraints can hinder seamless integration. Customized 

sensor deployment strategies and modular SHM systems are needed to bridge the gap between 

traditional infrastructure and modern monitoring technologies. 

E. High Initial Investment and ROI Concerns

Deploying a comprehensive IoT-based SHM system involves significant upfront costs, including 

sensors, communication networks, data platforms, and analytics tools. For many transportation 

agencies with constrained budgets, justifying the investment can be challenging—especially 

when the return on investment (ROI) is realized gradually through long-term cost savings and 

risk reduction. Demonstrating clear economic and safety benefits through pilot programs and 

lifecycle analyses is essential to drive adoption. 

Policy, Standards, and Governance

The successful adoption and sustainability of IoT-based Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) 

systems depend not only on technology but also on the presence of robust policy frameworks, 

standards, and governance models. These elements ensure system reliability, data integrity, 

stakeholder collaboration, and long-term scalability across different regions and infrastructure 

types. 

A. National and International SHM Standards (e.g., ISO 21939)

Standards play a vital role in guiding the design, deployment, and management of SHM systems. 

ISO 21939, for instance, outlines general requirements for SHM systems applied to civil 

engineering structures. It covers performance metrics, data management, and system reliability, 

helping ensure consistency and safety in deployments worldwide. National standards and 

guidelines (such as those from AASHTO in the U.S. or EN standards in the EU) complement 

these efforts by addressing region-specific needs, helping authorities and contractors ensure 

compliance and quality in smart infrastructure projects. 

B. Government Infrastructure Resilience Initiatives

Governments worldwide are increasingly recognizing the role of digital technologies in building 

infrastructure resilience. Through initiatives like the U.S. Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act 

(IIJA), the EU's Green Deal, or Africa’s Programme for Infrastructure Development (PIDA), 

funding is being allocated to enhance monitoring, maintenance, and sustainability of key 

infrastructure. These programs often emphasize the integration of IoT, AI, and data-driven tools 

to manage aging assets and mitigate risks from climate change, traffic overloads, or natural 

disasters. 

C. Public-Private Partnerships for Smart Infrastructure Projects

Given the high costs and technical complexity of deploying IoT-based SHM systems, public-

private partnerships (PPPs) are essential for pooling resources, expertise, and innovation. 

Governments can provide regulatory support and funding, while private sector stakeholders—

including technology firms, engineering companies, and telecom providers—can contribute 

technical solutions and infrastructure. Successful PPPs enable scalable SHM deployments, 

particularly in developing countries where state capacity may be limited. 

D. Open Data Policies and Interoperability Frameworks

As SHM systems generate vast amounts of data, open data policies and interoperability 

frameworks are crucial to promote transparency, innovation, and integration across platforms. 

Open data sharing enables researchers, developers, and infrastructure managers to build 

predictive tools, conduct cross-system analysis, and enhance situational awareness. 

Interoperability standards—such as those based on IoT protocols like MQTT, CoAP, or OPC 

UA—ensure seamless communication between devices from different vendors, preventing 

vendor lock-in and enabling system upgrades over time. 

Conclusion and Recommendations

A. Summary of IoT’s Transformative Role in SHM

The integration of the Internet of Things (IoT) into Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) is 

revolutionizing how bridges and highways are managed and maintained. By enabling real-time 

data collection, remote diagnostics, and predictive analytics, IoT empowers infrastructure 

stakeholders to detect issues early, enhance safety, and extend asset lifespans. From load 

monitoring and crack detection to environmental stress analysis, IoT-based SHM provides a 

continuous, data-driven understanding of structural conditions that traditional inspection methods 

cannot match. 

B. Emphasis on Proactive Infrastructure Management

IoT shifts the paradigm of infrastructure maintenance from reactive and scheduled inspections to 

proactive, condition-based strategies. Continuous monitoring allows for early identification of 

deterioration and risk, enabling timely interventions that prevent costly repairs and potential 

failures. This approach not only improves public safety but also reduces lifecycle costs and 

improves service reliability.

The Future of Structural Health Monitoring: Emerging Trends and Technologies
----------------------------------------------------------------------------

Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) is rapidly evolving, driven by advances in technology and the growing need to maintain the integrity of critical infrastructure. As urbanization intensifies and infrastructure ages, ensuring the safety and durability of structures like bridges, buildings, and dams has never been more crucial. At Pulse IoT Technologies, we are at the forefront of this transformation, leveraging cutting-edge IoT solutions to monitor and safeguard structural health. In this blog, we explore the emerging trends and technologies shaping the future of SHM.

**1. IoT-Enabled Real-Time Monitoring**

IoT is revolutionizing structural health monitoring by enabling real-time data collection and analysis. Traditional SHM methods often relied on periodic inspections and manual data logging, which can miss critical changes in structural integrity. IoT devices, such as concrete sensors and wireless strain gauges, provide continuous monitoring, allowing for the early detection of issues like corroded rebar or cracks in concrete. This real-time approach enhances the ability to predict and prevent failures, reducing the need for costly repairs and extending the lifespan of infrastructure.

**2. Advanced Data Analytics and Machine Learning**

The sheer volume of data generated by IoT-based SHM systems requires sophisticated data analytics to extract meaningful insights. Machine learning algorithms are increasingly being integrated into SHM platforms to identify patterns and predict structural issues before they become critical. For example, by analyzing data from structure monitoring systems, machine learning can predict the onset of concrete deterioration, enabling proactive maintenance strategies and more efficient allocation of resources.

**3. The Rise of Smart Materials**

Smart materials are an exciting development in the field of SHM. These materials can change their properties in response to environmental conditions, providing real-time feedback on the structural health of a building or bridge. For instance, self-sensing concrete incorporates sensors directly into the material, allowing for continuous monitoring of factors like stress, temperature, and moisture. This innovation not only enhances the accuracy of SHM but also simplifies the process of repairing concrete, as issues can be detected and addressed early.

**4. Integration of Drones and Robotics**

Drones and robotics are becoming integral tools in SHM, particularly for inspecting hard-to-reach areas or large-scale structures. Equipped with high-resolution cameras and sensors, drones can quickly and safely assess the condition of infrastructure, such as high-rise buildings or expansive bridges. Robotics, on the other hand, can be used to perform detailed inspections and even assist in concrete repair, particularly in hazardous environments. The use of drones and robotics not only improves the efficiency of SHM but also reduces the risks associated with manual inspections.

**5. Predictive Maintenance and Digital Twins**

Predictive maintenance, enabled by IoT and advanced analytics, is set to become a standard practice in

SHM. By continuously monitoring the health of a structure, it is possible to predict when and where

maintenance will be needed, preventing failures before they occur. This approach is closely linked to the

concept of digital twins—virtual replicas of physical structures that can simulate and predict their

behaviour under various conditions. Digital twins provide infrastructure owners with a powerful tool to

optimize maintenance schedules, reduce costs, and enhance the overall safety of their assets.

The future of structural health monitoring is bright, with IoT, smart materials, and advanced analytics

driving significant advancements. At Pulse IoT Technologies, we are committed to staying at the cutting

edge of these developments, providing innovative solutions that ensure the safety, durability, and

longevity of critical infrastructure. As the industry continues to evolve, embracing these emerging trends

and technologies will be key to maintaining the integrity of our built environment.

By integrating these innovations into your SHM strategy, you can proactively address structural issues,

reduce maintenance costs, and ensure the long-term safety of your infrastructure. Whether you're

dealing with concrete sensors, corroded rebar, or planning the repair of concrete, the future of SHM

promises more efficient, effective, and intelligent solutions.

Abstract
--------

Recent earthquakes worldwide have led to significant loss of life and structural damage to infrastructure, especially road bridges. Existing bridge monitoring systems have limitations, including restricted detection capabilities, subjectivity, human error, labor-intensive inspections, limited access to remote areas, and high costs. Aging infrastructures pose a critical concern for organizations and government funding policies, showing signs of decay and impending structural failure. To address these challenges, this research proposes an IoT-based bridge health status monitoring and warning system that is wireless, low-cost, durable, and user-friendly. The proposed system builds upon engineering standards and guidelines to classify bridge health status into categories ranging from excellent to collapse condition. It incorporates deflection, vibration, temperature, humidity, and infrared sensors, combined with IoT and a fuzzy logic algorithm. The primary objective is to reduce bridge maintenance costs, extend lifespans, and enhance transportation safety through an early warning system via a mobile application. Additionally, a Google Maps interface has been developed to display bridge conditions along with real-time traffic video. To validate the proposed system, a 3-D prototype model was constructed and tested. Practical testing of the fuzzy logic algorithm aligned with the simulation outcomes, demonstrating expected accuracy in determining bridge health status.

Keywords: 

[resilient infrastructure](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=resilient+infrastructure); [fuzzy logic](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=fuzzy+logic); [intelligent transport systems](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=intelligent+transport+systems); [internet of things](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=internet+of+things); [smart road bridges](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=smart+road+bridges); [structural health monitoring systems](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=structural+health+monitoring+systems); [UNSDGs](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=UNSDGs)

1. Introduction
---------------

Road bridge failures can have severe ramifications on transportation systems worldwide. Apart from the loss of lives and casualties, the disruption in services leads to highly adverse effects on economic growth. Throughout history, numerous bridge failures have resulted in tragic consequences, including human fatalities and substantial economic losses. A recent example is the collapse of Italy’s Morandi’s Polcevera Viaduct in 2018, resulting in 43 deaths and nine injuries as well as an estimated economic loss of approximately 100 million yuan (approximately 14 million dollars) [[1](https://www.mdpi.com/1424-8220/24/2/469#B1-sensors-24-00469)].

In the United States, according to the latest Report Card for America’s Infrastructure, 42% of the 617,000 country’s bridges are at least 50 years old; among these, 7.5% fall under the classification of “structurally deficient” [[2](https://www.mdpi.com/1424-8220/24/2/469#B2-sensors-24-00469)]. 

China, a country with massive bridge production, had a total number of 851,500 bridges by the end of 2018 [[3](https://www.mdpi.com/1424-8220/24/2/469#B3-sensors-24-00469)]. Despite this extensive infrastructure, the country has encountered several serious bridge collapses [[4](https://www.mdpi.com/1424-8220/24/2/469#B4-sensors-24-00469)]. Road bridge failures are attributed to a range of factors, including natural disasters, aging infrastructure, environmental conditions, deficiencies in structural design, material degradation due to environmental conditions, increased traffic loads, insufficient maintenance and inspection, and human error [[1](https://www.mdpi.com/1424-8220/24/2/469#B1-sensors-24-00469),[5](https://www.mdpi.com/1424-8220/24/2/469#B5-sensors-24-00469),[6](https://www.mdpi.com/1424-8220/24/2/469#B6-sensors-24-00469)]. 

Developing nations often face a myriad of challenges when it comes to bridge infrastructure development and monitoring. Some of the specific challenges include financial constraints; geopolitical and environmental factors; technology limitations; maintenance and upkeep; access to skilled labor; corruption and governance issues; inadequate planning; and, above all, impact on local sustained communities. Addressing these challenges requires a holistic approach involving technology development by academia and industry, government commitment, international support, improved governance, technology transfer, cap

enriched_points.expanded_content: IoT technologies enable continuous monitoring of structural integrity, allowing for early detection of issues, enhancing safety, and building resilience against environmental stresses and aging.

By integrating IoT sensors and energy-efficient technologies, monitoring practices become more sustainable, reducing resource consumption, enhancing data accuracy, and minimizing environmental impact.

Fostering collaboration between tech innovators and civil engineers promotes the development of advanced monitoring solutions, optimizing infrastructure management through shared expertise and resources.
